section.title: The Covenant Renewed
section.sectionRationale: God answers Moses' plea by proclaiming His own name in the fullest revelation of His character in the Old Testament, then renews the covenant broken by the golden calf. Moses returns with his face radiant from being in God's presence, so bright he must wear a veil before the people.
section.thesis: God proclaims His name as compassionate, gracious, and just, renews His covenant with Israel after the golden calf, and Moses descends with a face so radiant from being in God's presence that he must veil it before the people.
section.frameNote: Four movements: new tablets and God's proclamation of His own name and character; the terms of the renewed covenant; the command to write these words after forty days on the mountain; and Moses' radiant, veiled face.

units.label: The LORD proclaims His name
units.note: compassionate and gracious, slow to anger

media.alt: A figure bowed low before a cloud on a mountain
media.caption: The LORD passes before Moses and proclaims His own name and character.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Chisel out two stone tablets like the originals, and I will write on them the words that were on the first tablets, which you broke.
Be ready in the morning, and come up on Mount Sinai to present yourself before Me on the mountaintop.
No one may go up with you; in fact, no one may be seen anywhere on the mountain, not even the flocks or herds may graze in front of the mountain.”
So Moses chiseled out two stone tablets like the originals. He rose early in the morning, and taking the two stone tablets in his hands, he went up Mount Sinai as the LORD had commanded him.
And the LORD descended in a cloud, stood with him there, and proclaimed His name, the LORD.
Then the LORD passed in front of Moses and called out: “The LORD, the LORD God, is {{compassionate|compassionate and gracious}}, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion and faithfulness,

maintaining loving devotion to a thousand generations, forgiving iniquity, transgression, and sin. Yet He will by no means leave the guilty unpunished; He will visit the iniquity of the fathers on their children and grandchildren to the third and fourth generations.”
Moses immediately bowed down to the ground and worshiped.
“O Lord,” he said, “if I have indeed found favor in Your sight, my Lord, please go with us. Although this is a stiff-necked people, forgive our iniquity and sin, and take us as Your inheritance.”

units.label: The terms of the renewed covenant
units.note: make no treaty with the inhabitants of the land

verses.text: And the LORD said, “Behold, I am making a covenant. Before all your people I will perform wonders that have never been done in any nation in all the world. All the people among whom you live will see the LORD's work, for it is an awesome thing that I am doing with you.
Observe what I command you this day. I will drive out before you the Amorites, Canaanites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.
Be careful not to make a treaty with the inhabitants of the land you are entering, lest they become a snare in your midst.
Rather, you must tear down their altars, smash their sacred stones, and chop down their Asherah poles.
For you must not worship any other god, for the LORD, whose name is Jealous, is a jealous God.
Do not make a covenant with the inhabitants of the land, for when they prostitute themselves to their gods and sacrifice to them, they will invite you, and you will eat their sacrifices.
And when you take some of their daughters as brides for your sons, their daughters will prostitute themselves to their gods and cause your sons to do the same.
You shall make no molten gods for yourselves.

You are to keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread. For seven days at the appointed time in the month of Abib, you are to eat unleavened bread as I commanded you. For in the month of Abib you came out of Egypt.
The first offspring of every womb belongs to Me, including all the firstborn males among your livestock, whether cattle or sheep.

You must redeem the firstborn of a donkey with a lamb; but if you do not redeem it, you are to break its neck. You must redeem all the firstborn of your sons. No one shall appear before Me empty-handed.
Six days you shall labor, but on the seventh day you shall rest; even in the seasons of plowing and harvesting, you must rest.
And you are to celebrate the Feast of Weeks with the firstfruits of the wheat harvest, and the Feast of Ingathering at the turn of the year.
Three times a year all your males are to appear before the Lord GOD, the God of Israel.
For I will drive out the nations before you and enlarge your borders, and no one will covet your land when you go up three times a year to appear before the LORD your God.
Do not offer the blood of a sacrifice to Me along with anything leavened, and do not let any of the sacrifice from the Passover Feast remain until morning.
Bring the best of the firstfruits of your soil to the house of the LORD your God. You must not cook a young goat in its mother's milk.”
The LORD also said to Moses, “Write down these words, for in accordance with these words I have made a covenant with you and with Israel.”
So Moses was there with the LORD forty days and forty nights without eating bread or drinking water. He wrote on the tablets the words of the covenant, the Ten Commandments.

units.label: The radiant, veiled face
units.note: he was unaware his face had become radiant

media.alt: A veiled face descending a mountain
media.caption: Moses veils his radiant face after speaking with the LORD.

verses.text: And when Moses came down from Mount Sinai with the two tablets of the Testimony in his hands, he was unaware that his face had become radiant from speaking with the LORD.
Aaron and all the Israelites looked at Moses, and behold, his face was radiant. And they were afraid to approach him.
But Moses called out to them; so Aaron and all the leaders of the congregation returned to him, and Moses spoke to them.
And after this all the Israelites came near, and Moses commanded them to do everything that the LORD had told him on Mount Sinai.
When Moses had finished speaking with them, he put a {{veil|veil}} over his face.
But whenever Moses went in before the LORD to speak with Him, he would remove the veil until he came out. And when he came out, he would tell the Israelites what he had been commanded,
and the Israelites would see that the face of Moses was radiant. So Moses would put the veil back over his face until he went in to speak with the LORD.

compassionate.gloss: Compassionate and gracious, the opening words of the fullest self-revelation of God's character in the Old Testament. This proclamation becomes the most quoted description of God's nature throughout the rest of Scripture, especially in the Psalms and Prophets.

veil.gloss: A veil, covering Moses' radiant face after he had spoken with the LORD. Paul later contrasts this veil, which faded and had to be reapplied, with the unveiled, ever increasing glory believers now behold in Christ, 2 Corinthians 3:13-18.

basic.context: God tells Moses to chisel new tablets and returns to the mountain, where He passes before him and proclaims His own name and character in the fullest terms found anywhere in the Old Testament: compassionate, gracious, slow to anger, yet also just. God then renews the covenant with specific terms for life in the land, and Moses comes down with a face so radiant he must wear a veil before the people.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, historical renewal of the covenant after Israel's idolatry, not a repeatable ritual. God's proclaimed name in verses 6 and 7 functions as a genuine, foundational self-revelation that shapes the rest of the Bible's understanding of who God is, holding together mercy and justice without minimizing either.
basic.originalLanguages: God proclaims Himself rachum ve-chanun, compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, yet the same proclamation insists He will by no means leave the guilty unpunished. Moses' face becomes radiant from being in God's presence, requiring a masveh, veil, whenever he was not actively speaking with the people on God's behalf.
basic.typology: Moses' fading radiance, requiring a veil reapplied again and again, points to the temporary and partial nature of the old covenant's glory. Paul directly contrasts this with the greater, unfading glory believers behold in Christ, whose glory is not diminished or veiled from those who trust Him.
basic.christ: Paul in 2 Corinthians 3 explicitly interprets Moses' veil as a picture of a covenant that was fading, contrasted with the new covenant in Christ, whose glory believers behold with unveiled faces, being transformed into His likeness with ever increasing glory, 2 Corinthians 3:7-18. The very name proclaimed here, compassionate, gracious, forgiving, yet just, finds its full resolution at the cross, where God's justice and mercy meet.
basic.god: This proclamation of God's name is arguably Scripture's fullest self-description of His character, holding together compassion, patience, and forgiving love with an unwavering commitment to justice. God is neither a soft sentimentalist nor a cold judge; He is both merciful and holy at once, and this tension is resolved fully only at the cross.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Corinthians 3:7-18 interprets Moses' veil as picturing the fading old covenant, contrasted with believers' unveiled access to Christ's glory. Numbers 14:18 and Psalm 103:8 both quote this proclamation of God's name directly. Nehemiah 9:17 recalls it when Israel again faces judgment for sin.
basic.oneStory: After Israel's great betrayal, God proclaims His own name as both merciful and just, renews His covenant, and Moses' face shines with a fading glory that must be veiled. This partial, fading glory anticipates the permanent, unveiled glory believers now behold in Christ, in whom God's mercy and justice meet completely and forever.

context.literary: This chapter resolves the crisis begun with the golden calf: broken tablets are replaced, the covenant is explicitly renewed with terms echoing the earlier law, and the chapter closes with a striking physical sign, Moses' radiant face, of what it means to have been in God's presence.
context.historical: The renewed covenant terms specifically warn against alliances and intermarriage with the Canaanite nations Israel would soon encounter, addressing a real and pressing danger to Israel's exclusive worship of the LORD once they entered the land.
context.canonical: God's self-proclamation in verses 6 and 7 becomes the single most repeated description of His character throughout the rest of the Old Testament, quoted or echoed in Numbers, the Psalms, Jonah, Nehemiah, and Joel, functioning almost as Israel's creed about who God is.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record God's own definitive self-revelation of His character after Israel's idolatry and the covenant's renewal on that basis, showing that God's justice and mercy are both essential to who He is, not one at the expense of the other.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The covenant renewal terms are historically prescriptive for Israel entering Canaan, addressing specific dangers of that time and place; they are not directly prescriptive for the church today. The abiding truth is God's revealed character, compassionate and just, and its ultimate resolution in Christ.

debates.question: How do God's forgiving love and His visiting iniquity on children and grandchildren, verse 7, fit together?

views.label: Generational consequences, not inherited guilt
views.case: Many interpreters read this as describing the observable, natural consequences of sin patterns passed down through families and communities, not a claim that God punishes individuals for sins they did not personally commit, consistent with Ezekiel 18's insistence that each person is judged for their own sin.

views.label: Covenant solidarity within a household or nation
views.case: Others see this within the ancient near eastern understanding of corporate solidarity, where a household or nation could experience shared consequences of a leader's or community's sin, while still affirming, as the text itself does, that God's loving devotion to a thousand generations vastly outweighs judgment to the third or fourth.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the text emphasizes that God's mercy vastly outweighs His judgment in scope and duration, and neither reading understands this as contradicting personal responsibility for one's own sin, as later Scripture clarifies.

study.originalLanguages: God proclaims His own name using paired terms, rachum, compassionate, and chanun, gracious, alongside slow to anger and abounding in loving devotion, yet in the same breath affirms He will not leave the guilty unpunished, holding mercy and justice together without contradiction. Moses' face carries such radiance from this encounter that a masveh, veil, becomes necessary, a temporary covering for a temporary, fading glory.

typology.type: God's proclaimed name, compassionate and gracious yet fully just
typology.fulfillment: Fully resolved at the cross, where God's justice against sin and mercy toward sinners meet in Christ, Romans 3:25-26.
The greater, unfading glory believers now behold with unveiled faces in Christ, 2 Corinthians 3:18.

study.christ: Paul directly draws on this chapter in 2 Corinthians 3, contrasting the fading glory on Moses' veiled face with the permanent, unveiled glory believers now behold in Christ, being transformed from glory to glory. The character God proclaims here, patient, forgiving, yet unwilling to leave guilt unpunished, is not abandoned but fulfilled at the cross, where Christ bears the punishment sin deserves so that God can be both just and the justifier of everyone who has faith in Jesus, Romans 3:26.
study.god: This is arguably the fullest self-description of God's character in the entire Old Testament: compassionate, gracious, patient, abounding in loving devotion and faithfulness, forgiving, and yet unwilling to leave the guilty unpunished. God is neither indulgent nor merely severe; both His mercy and His justice are essential, unshakeable parts of who He is.

crossReferences.note: Interprets Moses' fading, veiled glory as pointing to the greater, unveiled glory believers behold in Christ.
Moses later quotes this same proclamation of God's name back to Him in intercession.
Echoes this exact language, compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion.

study.oneStory: After Israel's betrayal with the golden calf, God proclaims His own name as both deeply merciful and fully just, renews His covenant, and Moses returns radiant from His presence, needing a veil for glory that fades. That fading glory gives way to the permanent, unveiled glory of God revealed in Jesus Christ, in whom mercy and justice meet fully and forever.

quiz.q: What did God command Moses to bring up the mountain to replace the broken tablets?

options.text: Two stone tablets, chiseled by Moses himself, like the originals.
options.note: Verses 1 and 4 describe Moses chiseling out these replacement tablets.

options.text: A scroll made of papyrus.
options.note: The replacement was stone tablets, not a papyrus scroll.

options.text: Nothing; the original tablets were repaired.
options.note: The original tablets were shattered beyond repair in chapter 32; entirely new tablets were required.

options.text: No replacement is mentioned.
options.note: Verses 1 and 4 explicitly describe the new stone tablets Moses chiseled out.

quiz.q: How does God describe His own character in verses 6 to 7?

options.text: Compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in loving devotion, yet not leaving the guilty unpunished.
options.note: This is the exact proclamation recorded in verses 6 to 7.

options.text: Distant and unconcerned with human affairs.
options.note: The proclamation describes God as deeply compassionate and engaged, not distant or unconcerned.

options.text: Only merciful, with no mention of justice.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly balances mercy with the statement that God will not leave the guilty unpunished.

options.text: No description of God's character is given.
options.note: Verses 6 to 7 explicitly proclaim God's name and character in detail.

quiz.q: What specific danger did God warn Israel about regarding the nations in the land?

options.text: Making treaties and intermarrying with them, leading to idolatry.
options.note: Verses 12 to 16 describe this exact danger in detail.

options.text: Losing military battles against them.
options.note: The warning centers on spiritual compromise through treaties and intermarriage, not military defeat.

options.text: Adopting their system of weights and measures.
options.note: No such economic warning is given; the danger concerns idolatrous worship through alliance and intermarriage.

options.text: No warning is given about the nations.
options.note: Verses 12 through 16 explicitly warn against treaties and intermarriage leading to idolatry.

quiz.q: How long did Moses remain on the mountain this time, according to verse 28?

options.text: Forty days and forty nights, without eating bread or drinking water.
options.note: Verse 28 states this exact duration and fasting.

options.text: Seven days only.
options.note: Seven days describes the ordination period in chapter 29; this stay on Sinai lasted forty days and nights.

options.text: A single day and night.
options.note: The stay was far longer, forty days and nights, matching his earlier time on the mountain in chapter 24.

options.text: No duration is specified.
options.note: Verse 28 explicitly states forty days and forty nights.

quiz.q: Why did Moses begin wearing a veil over his face?

options.text: Because his face had become radiant from speaking with the LORD, and the Israelites were afraid to approach him.
options.note: Verses 29 to 33 describe both the radiance and the people's fear that led to the veil.

options.text: To hide a physical injury sustained on the mountain.
options.note: The text describes radiant glory from God's presence, not any injury, as the reason for the veil.

options.text: As a sign of mourning for the golden calf.
options.note: The veil specifically addressed his radiant face and the people's fear, not an act of mourning.

options.text: No reason is given for the veil.
options.note: Verses 29 through 33 explicitly explain the radiance and resulting need for a veil.

questions: God's proclaimed name holds together compassion and justice without contradiction. Which of these two truths about God do you find easier to believe, and which do you tend to neglect?
Moses immediately bowed down and worshiped after hearing God's name proclaimed. What would it look like for a fuller understanding of who God is to move you to worship rather than mere information?

The covenant renewal warned against gradual compromise through alliances and intermarriage with idolatrous nations. Where in your own life are small, ongoing compromises quietly pulling you away from wholehearted devotion to God?
Moses' face was radiant, yet he himself was unaware of it. Where have you seen evidence of God's work in your own life that others noticed before you did?

Moses' glory faded and needed to be veiled again and again, but believers now behold Christ's unfading glory with unveiled faces. How does knowing your access to God's glory in Christ never needs to be veiled change how boldly you approach Him?

translationNotes.note: This proclamation of God's name becomes the most frequently echoed description of God's character throughout the rest of the Old Testament, quoted or alluded to in Numbers 14:18, Psalm 86:15, Psalm 103:8, Joel 2:13, Jonah 4:2, and Nehemiah 9:17.

Visiting the iniquity of the fathers on their children and grandchildren likely describes observable generational consequences of sin rather than inherited guilt for another's sin, a point later clarified explicitly in Ezekiel 18:20.

His face had become radiant translates a Hebrew phrase related to the word for horn, qaran, which some ancient interpreters, including in certain classical artistic traditions, misunderstood as horns rather than rays of light; the context of radiance from God's presence makes clear that light, not horns, is intended.
